docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

I like to drink after I played

docs.title: Code examples
docs.text:
An example of a codeblock for Python:

add_numbs.py
# Function to add two numbers
def add_two_numbers(num1, num2):
    return num1 + num2

# Example usage
result = add_two_numbers(5, 3)
print('The sum is:', result)

Example codeblock for Javascript with lines highlighted:

concat_strings.js
// Function to concatenate two strings
function concatenateStrings(str1, str2) {
  return str1 + str2;
}

// Example usage
const result = concatenateStrings("Hello, ", "World!");
console.log("The concatenated string is:", result);

graph LR
  A[Start] --> B{Failure?};
  B -->|Yes| C[Investigate...];
  C --> D[Debug];
  D --> B;
  B ---->|No| E[Success!];

docs.title: Sequence Diagrams
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
  autonumber
  Server->>Terminal: Send request
  loop Health
      Terminal->>Terminal: Check for health
  end
  Note right of Terminal: System online
  Terminal-->>Server: Everything is OK
  Terminal->>Database: Request customer data
  Database-->>Terminal: Customer data

docs.title: Signing Proxmox VE with a Windows AD CS Certificate
docs.text: How-to for issuing a trusted SSL cert to a single Proxmox node from a single-tier Windows AD Certificate Authority.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Node: pve-0 (FQDN pve-0.homelab.local, IP 192.168.16.235)
  • CA: single-tier AD CS on Windows Server 2016 DC (homelab-DC-CA)
  • Goal: trusted padlock on https://pve-0.homelab.local:8006

Note

Adjust names, IPs, and CA host to match your own setup.

docs.title: Part 1: On pve-0 (generate key and CSR)
1. Create the OpenSSL config
docs.text:
Open a new CMD prompt, then connect to your Proxmox VE server via SSH.

ssh root@192.168.16.235
Create the OpenSSL config file.

nano /root/cert.cfg

Using the example provided below, copy and paste the contents from the example below into the cert.cfg file you created. Be sure to make the neccessary adjustments to match your environment needs.

[req]
distinguished_name = req_distinguished_name
req_extensions = v3_req
prompt = no

[req_distinguished_name]
C = US
ST = Georgia
L = Atlanta
O = HomeLab
OU = IT
CN = pve-0.'homelab.local'

[v3_req]
subjectAltName = @alt_names

[alt_names]
DNS.1 = pve-0.'homelab.local'
DNS.2 = pve-0
IP.1 = '192.168.16.235'

Once you have made the neccessary changes you will then save the changes and overwrite the file (Ctrl+X + Enter).

docs.title: 2. Generate the private key
docs.text: openssl genrsa -out /etc/pve/nodes/pve-0/pveproxy-ssl.key 2048

docs.title: 3. Generate the CSR
docs.text: openssl req -new -key /etc/pve/nodes/pve-0/pveproxy-ssl.key -out /tmp/pve-0.csr -config /root/cert.cfg

docs.title: 4. Print the CSR so you can copy it
docs.text:
cat /tmp/pve-0.csr

Copy the whole block, including the BEGIN and END lines.

docs.title: Part 2: On the Domain Controller (issue the cert)
5. Submit the CSR
docs.text:
Web enrollment (easiest if installed):

  1. Browse to http://localhost/certsrv
  2. Request a certificate > advanced certificate request
  3. Paste the CSR into the Saved Request box
  4. Certificate Template: Web Server
  5. Submit
  6. Choose Base 64 encoded, Download certificate, save as pve-0.crt

Or from an elevated Command Prompt:

certreq -submit -attrib "CertificateTemplate:WebServer" pve-0.csr pve-0.crt

Note

Submit to the same CA that will issue the chain (here, the homelab-DC CA).

docs.title: 6. Export the root CA cert as Base64
docs.text:
certutil -ca.cert C:\root-ca-der.crt
certutil -encode C:\root-ca-der.crt C:\root-ca.pem

Open C:\root-ca.pem in Notepad. It must start with -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----.

Why Base64 matters

The root CA comes out as DER (binary) by default, and binary does not survive copy-paste through a text editor. Convert it to Base64 (PEM) on the DC first so it stays intact.

You now have two text files on the DC: pve-0.crt and root-ca.pem.

docs.title: Part 3: Move both certs to pve-0
docs.text: Both files are Base64 text now, so copy-paste is safe.

docs.title: 7. Paste the leaf cert
docs.text:
nano /tmp/pve-0.crt

Paste, save.

docs.title: 8. Paste the root CA cert
docs.text:
nano /tmp/root-ca.pem

Paste, save.

docs.title: 9. Confirm both are valid PEM
docs.text:
head -1 /tmp/pve-0.crt
head -1 /tmp/root-ca.pem

Both must show -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----.

Never move binary through an editor

If either file shows garbled characters, it got mangled in transit. Re-export as Base64 and paste again. Do not move the DER/binary version through a text editor, the bytes get re-encoded and the cert becomes unreadable.

docs.title: Part 4: Assemble and apply on pve-0
10. Build the chained pem (leaf on top, root below)
docs.text: cat /tmp/pve-0.crt /tmp/root-ca.pem > /etc/pve/nodes/pve-0/pveproxy-ssl.pem

docs.title: 11. Verify the SAN made it into the cert
docs.text:
openssl x509 -in /tmp/pve-0.crt -noout -text | grep -A1 "Subject Alternative Name"

You want to see pve-0.homelab.local (plus the short name and IP if you kept them).

docs.title: 12. Confirm the key and cert are a matched pair
docs.text:
openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in /etc/pve/nodes/pve-0/pveproxy-ssl.pem | openssl md5
openssl rsa  -noout -modulus -in /etc/pve/nodes/pve-0/pveproxy-ssl.key | openssl md5

Warning

Both hashes must be identical. If they differ, the cert and key are from different generations. Regenerate from Part 1.

docs.title: 13. (Optional) Verify the chain validates against the root
docs.text:
openssl verify -CAfile /tmp/root-ca.pem /tmp/pve-0.crt

You want pve-0.crt: OK.

docs.title: 14. Restart the proxy
docs.text: systemctl restart pveproxy

docs.title: 15. DNS
docs.text: Confirm the DC has an A record for pve-0 to 192.168.16.235 in the homelab.local forward & lookup zone.

docs.title: 16. Test
docs.text:
From a domain-joined machine (already trusts the AD root CA via Group Policy), browse to:

https://pve-0.homelab.local:8006

Clean padlock means done.

  • Name-mismatch warning: you used the wrong name. Use the FQDN.
  • Untrusted warning: that machine does not have your AD root CA in its trust store. On a non-domain machine, import root-ca.pem into Trusted Root Certification Authorities manually.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Certs bind to names, not ports

Certs bind to hostnames and IPs (SAN), never to ports. The cert covers pve-0.homelab.local on any port, including 8006.

  • The cert files Proxmox reads are fixed paths:
    • /etc/pve/nodes/pve-0/pveproxy-ssl.pem (leaf + chain)
    • /etc/pve/nodes/pve-0/pveproxy-ssl.key (private key)
  • On a cluster, repeat per node. Each node needs its own cert with that node's FQDN/IP in the SAN.
  • Default cert lifetime from the Web Server template is typically 1 to 2 years. Set a reminder to repeat this before expiry.
